---
title: "Day 3 — Exercise: A codon table with `Option`"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Write `translate_codon(codon: [u8; 3]) -> Option<u8>`: given three DNA bases, return the corresponding amino acid as a single ASCII letter, or `None` if the codon is unrecognised (typically because it contains an `N` or other ambiguity character).

Stop codons (`TAA`, `TAG`, `TGA`) return `Some(b'*')` — they *are* recognised, they just mean "end translation here".

## Background

The standard genetic code maps each of the 64 possible DNA triplets to one of 20 amino acids or a stop. Translation is the inner loop of every protein-coding-gene analysis. A robust translator returns:

- `Some(amino_acid)` for the 61 sense codons
- `Some(b'*')` for the 3 stop codons
- `None` for everything else (anything containing N, IUPAC ambiguity codes, lower-case characters mixed with garbage, etc.)

[`Option<T>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/option/enum.Option.html) is Rust's "this might be there or not" type. Unlike `null` in many other languages, the compiler forces every caller to handle both cases — there is no way to accidentally use a `None` as if it were a value.

![The standard genetic code: every cell is indexed by its first base (row band), second base (column band), and third base (sub-row). Background colour groups amino acids by chemistry — hydrophobic, polar uncharged, acidic, basic, or stop. `ATG` is the universal start codon.](images/codon-table.svg){#fig-codon-table fig-alt="A 4 by 4 grid of outer cells indexed by first base (rows T, C, A, G) and second base (columns T, C, A, G). Each outer cell contains four sub-rows for the third base T, C, A, G. Every sub-row shows a codon on the left and its one-letter amino acid on the right. Cells are background-coloured: cream for hydrophobic amino acids, light blue for polar uncharged, light pink for acidic, light green for basic, and dark grey with white text for stop codons. The codon ATG is annotated as the start codon. A legend at the bottom names the colour categories."}

## The exercise

```bash
cd day3/ex-codon-table
```

Implement the full standard codon table. Make all nine tests pass.

## How to run

```bash
cargo test
cargo run -- ATG    # M
cargo run -- TAA    # * (stop)
cargo run -- ATN    # None  (contains an N)
cargo run -- atg    # M     (case-insensitive)
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — normalising case"}
The standard codon table is given in upper case. Convert each byte with [`u8::to_ascii_uppercase`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.u8.html#method.to_ascii_uppercase) at the start so the match itself only handles upper case:

```rust
let upper = [
    codon[0].to_ascii_uppercase(),
    codon[1].to_ascii_uppercase(),
    codon[2].to_ascii_uppercase(),
];
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — matching on a byte array"}
[Byte string literals](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/tokens.html#byte-string-literals) `b"ATG"` have type `&[u8; 3]`. Inside [`match`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch06-02-match.html) patterns, they match an array of bytes — so you can write:

```rust
match upper {
    b"ATG" => Some(b'M'),
    // ...
    _ => None,
}
```

Wait — `upper` has type `[u8; 3]` and the pattern `b"ATG"` is `&[u8; 3]`. Won't the types mismatch? In modern Rust they do not: match patterns auto-dereference. (If you ever get a type error here, you can always write `match &upper { b"ATG" => ... }` to be explicit.) See [the Rust reference on match ergonomics](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/patterns.html#binding-modes).
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — combining patterns with `|`"}
Most amino acids correspond to several codons (this is the genetic code's redundancy). Use [`|` alternatives](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/patterns.html#or-patterns) to group them on a single arm:

```rust
b"TTA" | b"TTG" | b"CTT" | b"CTC" | b"CTA" | b"CTG" => Some(b'L'),  // Leucine
b"TAA" | b"TAG" | b"TGA" => Some(b'*'),                              // Stop
```

The catch-all `_ => None` at the end handles every codon containing an `N` (or other non-ACGT character) — anything we did not list explicitly.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 4 — the full table"}
The 20 standard amino acids (each followed by its codon set):

| AA | Codons |
|----|--------|
| F  | TTT TTC |
| L  | TTA TTG CTT CTC CTA CTG |
| I  | ATT ATC ATA |
| M  | ATG |
| V  | GTT GTC GTA GTG |
| S  | TCT TCC TCA TCG AGT AGC |
| P  | CCT CCC CCA CCG |
| T  | ACT ACC ACA ACG |
| A  | GCT GCC GCA GCG |
| Y  | TAT TAC |
| *  | TAA TAG TGA |
| H  | CAT CAC |
| Q  | CAA CAG |
| N  | AAT AAC |
| K  | AAA AAG |
| D  | GAT GAC |
| E  | GAA GAG |
| C  | TGT TGC |
| W  | TGG |
| R  | CGT CGC CGA CGG AGA AGG |
| G  | GGT GGC GGA GGG |

That is 64 codons total — full coverage of every plain ACGT triplet.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
The full match is long but mechanical:

```rust
fn translate_codon(codon: [u8; 3]) -> Option<u8> {
    let upper = [
        codon[0].to_ascii_uppercase(),
        codon[1].to_ascii_uppercase(),
        codon[2].to_ascii_uppercase(),
    ];
    match &upper {
        b"TTT" | b"TTC" => Some(b'F'),
        b"TTA" | b"TTG" | b"CTT" | b"CTC" | b"CTA" | b"CTG" => Some(b'L'),
        b"ATT" | b"ATC" | b"ATA" => Some(b'I'),
        b"ATG" => Some(b'M'),
        b"GTT" | b"GTC" | b"GTA" | b"GTG" => Some(b'V'),
        b"TCT" | b"TCC" | b"TCA" | b"TCG" | b"AGT" | b"AGC" => Some(b'S'),
        b"CCT" | b"CCC" | b"CCA" | b"CCG" => Some(b'P'),
        b"ACT" | b"ACC" | b"ACA" | b"ACG" => Some(b'T'),
        b"GCT" | b"GCC" | b"GCA" | b"GCG" => Some(b'A'),
        b"TAT" | b"TAC" => Some(b'Y'),
        b"TAA" | b"TAG" | b"TGA" => Some(b'*'),
        b"CAT" | b"CAC" => Some(b'H'),
        b"CAA" | b"CAG" => Some(b'Q'),
        b"AAT" | b"AAC" => Some(b'N'),
        b"AAA" | b"AAG" => Some(b'K'),
        b"GAT" | b"GAC" => Some(b'D'),
        b"GAA" | b"GAG" => Some(b'E'),
        b"TGT" | b"TGC" => Some(b'C'),
        b"TGG" => Some(b'W'),
        b"CGT" | b"CGC" | b"CGA" | b"CGG" | b"AGA" | b"AGG" => Some(b'R'),
        b"GGT" | b"GGC" | b"GGA" | b"GGG" => Some(b'G'),
        _ => None,
    }
}
```

Full reference in `day3/ex-codon-table-solution/src/main.rs`.
:::

## Where this leads

- **Day 4**: translate every record of a FASTA file using this function, returning the protein sequence (truncated at the first stop codon).
- A nice property to think about: stops are *encoded* as a regular `Some(b'*')` value rather than as `None`. This separates "unknown codon" (`None`) from "end of translation" (`Some(b'*')`) — two different semantics that the type system now keeps straight for you.
